---
title: "Phase B — Fee vs. Principal Simulator"
subtitle: "Interactive companion to the research note"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

## Overview

This notebook is the **Phase B** companion to `research-note.md`. It reproduces
the §2–§5 closed-form P&L moments, estimates the §4 tail-risk metrics by Monte
Carlo, and lets the reader explore the `(α, μ, σ)` grid interactively. All
Monte Carlo computation happens in TypeScript (`src/`); this page only reads
the emitted JSON and visualises it.

Regenerate the data before previewing:

```sh
npm run simulate -- --seed 42
npm run sweep -- --seed 42
```

::: {.callout-note-lite}
**What you will see below.** Four kinds of plots: (1) a scorecard table of
closed-form vs Monte Carlo P&L moments with tail metrics, (2) overlaid P&L
distributions for the three stochastic models, (3) diagnostics on the shared
random kernel `I_T := ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt`, and (4) parameter sweeps showing how tail
risk and the break-even quote `Q*` respond to drift `μ`, volatility `σ`, and
hedge ratio `α`.
:::

### Notation cheat-sheet

A brief reminder of the symbols used throughout (full derivations in
`research-note.md` §1–§5):

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `S_t` | kVCM / USD spot price at time `t` | USD / kVCM |
| `P` | protocol price (constant) | kVCM / tonne |
| `π_t := P·S_t` | USD protocol price per tonne | USD / tonne |
| `λ` | deterministic retirement flow | tonnes / year |
| `T` | horizon | years |
| `N := λ·T` | total tonnes retired over `[0,T]` | tonnes |
| `f` | fee rate | dimensionless |
| `Q` | fixed USD quote per tonne (principal model) | USD / tonne |
| `α ∈ [0,1]` | pre-purchase (hedge) fraction | dimensionless |
| `I_T := ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt` | time-integral of GBM, the shared random kernel | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| `R_fee = f·P·λ·I_T` | fee-based revenue | USD |
| `Π_b2b = Q·N − P·λ·I_T` | back-to-back (α=0) P&L | USD |
| `Π_matched = N·(Q − P·S_0)` | matched pre-purchase (α=1), deterministic | USD |
| `Π_α = (1−α)·Π_b2b + α·Π_matched` | partial pre-purchase (α∈[0,1]) | USD |
| `Q* = (1+f)·P·S_0·(e^{μT}−1)/(μT)` | break-even quote equating `E[Π_b2b] = E[R_fee]` | USD / tonne |

## Single-run outputs

Loaded from `data/run-42.json` — produced by `npm run simulate -- --seed 42`.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

### Run parameters

The scorecard below echoes the parameters used for this run. `Q*` is the
closed-form break-even quote — if `Q` matches `Q*` the fee-based and
back-to-back principal books have equal expected P&L (but not equal
distributions, per §5).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (hedge ratio)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### §4 — P&L moments: closed-form vs Monte Carlo

Each row is one of the four models from the research note. The first block
of columns cross-checks the Monte Carlo mean against the Dufresne-based
closed form: `±CI95` is the ±95% half-width of the MC mean, and `z` is
`(MC − closed) / stderr`. For large `nPaths` the MC mean should land within
its own CI and `|z|` should rarely exceed 2. The second block of columns
reports the risk quantities the closed form does not cover — empirical VaR,
CVaR, loss probability, and the Sharpe-like ratio `E[Π] / SD[Π]`. All P&L
numbers are USD over the horizon `T`.

<div class="legend-table">

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| `E[Π] (cf) / (mc)` | expected P&L — closed form vs Monte Carlo |
| `±CI95` | ±95% half-width of the MC mean |
| `SD (cf) / (mc)` | standard deviation of P&L |
| `z` | `(MC−cf) / stderr`; MC sanity check |
| `VaR95 / VaR99` | loss threshold exceeded with 5% / 1% probability |
| `CVaR95 / CVaR99` | expected loss in the worst 5% / 1% tail |
| `P[Π<0]` | probability of a losing realisation |
| `Sharpe` | `E[Π] / SD[Π]`, invariant under `f`-scaling for fee model |

</div>

`principal_3a` is deterministic at horizon, so SD, CI95, and z are zero by
construction; VaR/CVaR simply reflect the sign of the matched P&L.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => x.toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => x.toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => x.toFixed(2),
    var95: x => x.toFixed(2),
    var99: x => x.toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => x.toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => x.toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : x.toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### P&L distributions

Each panel is one stochastic model's empirical density of terminal P&L,
from a sub-sampled Monte Carlo cloud. The vertical black rule is the
closed-form mean `E[Π]`; the dashed grey rule is `Π = 0`, so the mass to
the left of it is the loss probability reported in the table.

- **Fee book** (`R_fee = f·P·λ·I_T`): non-negative by construction, so mass
  sits entirely in the right half-plane. Distribution is right-skewed — a
  rescaled image of the `I_T` distribution.
- **Principal 3b** (back-to-back, α = 0): linear *decreasing* in `I_T`, so
  the same kernel produces a left-skewed loss tail. Upside is capped at
  `Q·N` (reached as `S_t → 0`), downside is unbounded.
- **Principal 3c** (partial, α = 0.5 in this run): variance scales as
  `(1−α)²` relative to 3b, so the density is narrower.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pnlData = [
  ...run.sampleTraces.fee.map(v => ({model: "fee", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.b2b.map(v => ({model: "principal_3b", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.partial.map(v => ({model: "principal_3c", pnl: v}))
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L Π  (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "~s" },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  fy: { label: "model" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"], label: "model" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlData, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "model", fy: "model", thresholds: 60 })),
    Plot.ruleX(run.rows.filter(r => ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"].includes(r.name)),
      { x: "closedFormMean", fy: "name", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black rule on each panel: closed-form `E[Π]`. Dashed grey rule:
break-even `Π = 0`._

### I_T histogram vs. Dufresne closed-form moments

`I_T := ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt` is the shared random kernel driving both the fee book
and the principal back-to-back book. Its first two moments are known in
closed form (Dufresne, 2001), but the distribution itself is not log-normal,
so tail quantities must be estimated numerically. This plot is a Monte Carlo
histogram of `I_T` — the solid rule verifies that the empirical mean matches
Dufresne's `E[I_T] = S_0·(e^{μT}−1)/μ`; the dashed rules sit at `E[I_T] ± SD`.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: `E[I_T]` (Dufresne closed form). Dashed black: `E[I_T] ± SD[I_T]`._

### Sample GBM paths — matched-book NAV view (§3a)

The Phase A note defines the mark-to-market inventory value for the fully
matched pre-purchase book (α = 1) as

```
V_t = (N · P) · (1 − t/T) · S_t.
```

This is a scaled GBM attenuated by the linear burn schedule. `V_0 = N·P·S_0`
is the purchase cost; `V_T = 0` because inventory has been fully retired by
the horizon. The plot shows a handful of sampled paths of `V_t` — the
highlighted orange line is the first path, the grey lines are the rest.
Interim drawdowns in `V_t` are the solvency concern even though terminal
P&L is deterministic.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Grey lines: sampled Monte Carlo NAV paths. Orange line: the first path,
highlighted for visibility. `V_T` is pinned to zero by the burn schedule
regardless of `S_T`._

### NAV shortfall vs. schedule (§3a)

For the matched book, a meaningful solvency statistic is the worst
_shortfall against the expected decay schedule_:

```
D := max_{t ≤ T}  [ N·P·(1 − t/T) · (S_0 − S_t) ]₊
```

i.e., the deepest excursion of `V_t` below its pre-hedged deterministic
path. This histogram is the empirical distribution of `D` across paths.
Values on the x-axis are USD losses relative to schedule.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "~s" },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed black: 95th percentile of the shortfall distribution. Solid black:
99th percentile. These are the natural stress thresholds for the matched
book even though its terminal P&L is deterministic._

## Parameter sweep

Loaded from `data/sweep.json` — a grid of Monte Carlo runs over
`(α, μ, σ)`. Use the controls below to slice the grid. The plots below
track `CVaR95` for the **principal 3c** (partial pre-purchase) book:
CVaR95 is the expected loss in the worst 5% of outcomes, so larger values
are worse. All P&L sweep quantities are in USD.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "hedge ratio α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: 0.05, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility σ  (fixed α, μ)

Holding `α` and `μ` fixed at the values chosen above, this line shows how
the CVaR95 of the 3c book grows with volatility `σ`. The relationship is
roughly monotone: more kVCM vol → a fatter left tail on P&L.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Sensitivity of the 3c tail to `σ` at the selected `(α, μ)`. A monotone rise
is the expected signature — vol directly inflates `Var[I_T]` and hence the
tail of `Π_α`._

### Tail risk vs hedge ratio α  (fixed μ, σ at grid[1])

This slice fixes `μ` at the selected value and `σ` at the **second** grid
value, then sweeps `α` from 0 (fully back-to-back) to 1 (fully matched).
Since `Var[Π_α] = (1−α)² · Var[Π_b2b]`, the CVaR95 curve should collapse
quadratically toward zero as `α → 1` — this is the visual confirmation
that pre-purchasing inventory is the natural hedge for the back-to-back
book.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Hedge ratio α vs tail risk. Fixed μ (selected above), σ = second grid value. At α = 1 the book is deterministic so CVaR95 collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "hedge ratio α   (0 = back-to-back, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## Break-even quote surface Q*(μ, T)

`Q*` is the USD quote that equalises the *expected* P&L of the
back-to-back principal book with the fee-based book:

```
Q* = (1 + f) · P · S_0 · (e^{μT} − 1) / (μ T).
```

Normalising by `(1+f)·P·S_0` removes the trivial scaling and leaves only
the drift-horizon shape `(e^{μT}−1)/(μT)`. The dashed horizontal rule is
the `μ = 0` limit: at zero drift, `Q* = (1+f)·P·S_0`, i.e. the fee-based
time-zero quote.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_Curves for each drift `μ` in the grid. Positive drift lifts `Q*` above the
fee-based quote (principal must charge more to compensate for expected
kVCM appreciation); negative drift pushes it below. The `μ = 0` curve
collapses onto the dashed unit line._

## Reading notes

- All closed-form / Monte Carlo agreement checks live in the `z` column of
  the §4 table — a single glance should tell you whether the MC is
  well-converged at the current `nPaths`.
- The fee and back-to-back books share the *same* random kernel `I_T`, so
  variance ratios are exact algebraic multiples (`(P/f)²`) — deviations
  would indicate a simulator bug.
- This notebook is Phase B; Phase C extensions (jump diffusion, Poisson
  demand, historical calibration, dynamic hedging) are intentionally out
  of scope here.
